content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What’s the best hidden gem you randomly discovered and loved?

**Score:** 112
**Body:** Some of the best movie experiences come from stumbling across something you didn’t expect it could be a film you found while scrolling late at night, something recommended by accident or even a random DVD or streaming pick that ended up sticking with you. Those hidden gems often end up being more memorable than the big blockbusters. What’s the best hidden gem movie you randomly discovered and ended up loving?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Suicide Kings. A recommendation from my local video store owner back in the day
2. Seeking a Friend for the End of the World. It's a movie you expect to be unbelievably funny that ends up being incredibly sweet as well
3. Palm Springs. I saw the cover online and saw Andy Samberg and thought 'oh nice a comedy this should be good'. It was not what I expected and was actually amazing
  - I’ve been binging Brooklyn 99 and I’m basically in love with Andy. Gotta look this one up
4. Take Shelter
5. Big Trouble In Little China
6. I once went to the cinema intending to rewatch *Field of Dreams*, which I had first seen the previous week at the same cinema and really enjoyed so wanted to see it again. I bought the ticket and went to the same theatre at the multiplex it had screened in the previous week. A strange, experimental movie started, with no dialogue, off-kilter camera angles and heavily filtered photography, about a middle aged couple seeming to have a fight on the side of a road in the desert. I figured it was just a short film before the movie, this was back when short films were sometimes screened before features, so I didn't think much of it. In the film, the husband drives off, leaving the woman behind with just a suitcase and, as she wanders off down the desert road, music begins to play and credits appear as I gradually realised that this was the feature film, and I was in the wrong theatre!

I thought about getting up and going to the right theatre but the film's theme song was really beautiful and the set-up intrigued me, so I decided to stick it out and watch this "mystery movie". It was the best mistake I ever made in a cinema; the movie was *Bagdad Cafe*, and it was so good I actually came back the following week and watched, on purpose this time, again!
7. The man from earth
8. Had never heard of Carlitos Way. I’m an American and was living in Bogota Colombia at the time. I stayed up late watching TV and it started and I thought it looked interesting….watched the whole thing in Spanish and thought “whoa, that was amazing. Everyone should see this.” Then I learned of its popularity. Needless to say, still a favorite of mine.
9. A Dark Song.

## 2. whats a movie you can watch over and over again and still find all the jokes funny

**Score:** 66
**Body:** ill start inbetweeners 1 and 2 i think its the pure immaturity and stupidity of them but they just never fail to crack me up same goes for wolf of wallstreet

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Young Frankenstein (1974)
2. Superbad. so many underrated moments too beyond the popular ones
3. Spaceball’s jokes only get more relevant
4. Drop Dead Gorgeous 
5. The Blues Brothers
6. Airplane!, Blazing Saddles, Monty Python and the Holy Grail.
7. Monty Python and the Holy Grail.
8. My Cousin Vinnie
9. The Princess Bride!!!!
10. *Real Genius*

## 3. Please suggest the best written movie of all time!

**Score:** 49
**Body:** My favorite movies are Fargo, Chinatown, 2001: A Space Odyssey, 12 Angry Men, Pulp Fiction, The Sting, To Kill A Mockingbird, The Apartment, and LA Confidential. What really made me love all these movies so much is their writing, it's just so fucking good. I'd define good writing as concise, I'm a huge fan of show don't tell movies, as well as movies that don't waste a single line of dialogue or second of its runtime. I also think a deep, philosophical theme or message is something that's required. I want a movie that I can meditate on and write a whole ass PhD dissertation on both the quality of writing as well as the philosophical ideas presented in them. I'm open to all genres and have no triggers, I just really fucking love watching good movies. Thank you so much in advance!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think In Bruges is the perfect screenplay.
2. Casablanca is so quotable because it’s so well written. It’s
3. Sunset Boulevard 
  - "I am big. It's the pictures that got small"
4. Glengarry Glenross
5. The Big Lebowski
6. Moonstruck.
7. Princess Bride
8. The “Before” trilogy by Richard Linklater - Before Sunrise / Before Sunset / Before Midnight
9. It's In The Mood For Love. Incredibly intricate exhibition of what's it's like when adults fall in love instead of just kids. It's really unmatched in that sort of craftsmanship.

## 4. Any suggestions for feel good or uplifting movies I can watch with my parents?

**Score:** 26
**Body:** They like movies with good morals like You’ve Got Mail, Roman Holiday, An Unfinished Life… Anything with a similar mood. Little conflict, happy endings, preferably mainstream but not necessary.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Second Hand Lions.  Duval gives a great speech in it.
2. Secondhand Lions and Hunt for the Wilderpeople
  - Hunt for the Wilderpeople is my any-age, any-taste, any-mood movie recommendation.
3. Babe.
4. Big Fish
5. Bringing up baby.  1938 Katherine Hepburn and Cary grant.  It is so much fun

Thoroughly modern millie. 1967 Julie Andrews and Mary Tyler moore
6. Stranger Than Fiction
7. My Big Fat Greek Wedding
8. Field of Dreams. It’s such gentle fun up until the last ten minutes, and those ten minutes leave everyone in tears.
9. Big Fish

## 5. Quirky indie movies that stick with you

**Score:** 23
**Body:** I love indie films that feel unique or slightly offbeat, like *Little Miss Sunshine* or *Moonrise Kingdom*. They’re charming, funny, and sometimes heartbreaking. Any indie gems you’d recommend that are memorable, creative, and not overly mainstream?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Amelie *(2001) - French*

Bagdad Cafe *(1987)*

Come Back to the 5 and Dime, Jimmy Dean *(1982)*

Hairspray *(1988)*

Harold and Maud *(1971)*

I ♥ Huckabees *(2004)* 

Run Lola Run *(1998)*

To Die For *(1995)*

Twice Upon a Time *(1983) - animated*

What the Bleep do We Know? *(2004)*
2. Hunt for the Wilderpeople
3. Captain Fantastic 
4. - The Station Agent (2003)

- Dinner in America (2020)

- Me and Earl and the dying girl (2015)

- Cashback (2006)

- Palm Springs (2020)

- Obvious Child (2014)

- Submarine (2010)

- Shiva Baby (2020)
5. Booksmart, I really loved that one, so indie and very comfort show coded
6. Lars and the real girl
7. Ghost World, Welcome to the Dollhouse, Kids, Party Monster
8. The Florida Project

Dinner in America
9. The Way, Way Back. 

Stellar cast having a blast. 

Steve Carell, Toni Collette, Allison Janney, AnnaSophia Robb, Sam Rockwell, and Maya Rudolph, with Rob Corddry, Amanda Peet.
10. The Way, Way Back 

The Florida Project

Eighth Grade

Lars and the Real Girl

Ruby Sparks 

Cyrus

Jeff Who Lives At Home

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Best comedic moment in a serious scene.

**Score:** 7903
**Body:** This scene in Wolverine was pretty funny.


### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "We're leaving" - Event Horizon
  - "Fuck this ship!"
  - god that shit is too funny

*30 seconds of unspeakable horrifying blood orgy, torture, murder, rape, capped off by dude who ripped his eyes out breaking the fourth wall*

……. “we’re leaving.”

But also, I love how fucking real that is. Fuck that video, fuck this place, fuck you for brining us here, and fuck space in general: we out.
  - Totally catches you off guard how funny that line comes across
2. In ConAir, when a guy yells at Cyrus "Cy!" and he responds "onara" and proceeds to set him on fire.
3. I also love the time where Prof X and Mags came into the bar and Wolvie told them to "fuck off."
  - Truly one of the best cameos
4. "Leave the gun. Take the cannoli"



-The Godfather
5. “I’m the guy who does his job, you must be the other guy.”  

Either that, or Mr. Wolfe enjoying his coffee.
6. Canadian and American dog tags are very different. I think it was the first Xmen movie that showed wolverine’s tags and they were the Canadian style. That was a nice little touch of detail.

## 2. Name a film that’s so beautifully shot it feels like a painting.

**Score:** 797

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Blade runner 2049
2. Barry Lyndon
3. 1917 tbh
4. The Assassination of Jesse James by the Coward Robert Ford
5. The Grand Budapest Hotel
6. Last of the Mohicans.
7. Rogue One for me. 

Landscapes
8. Lawrence of Arabia
9. The Fall (2006)
10. LOTR

## 3. The Unofficial Trilogy.

**Score:** 492
**Body:** Rush (2013)
Ford v Ferrari (2019)
F1 (2025)

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "the 3 pretty good and relatively recent racing movies" trilogy?
2. Where the hell is Talladega Nights
  - OP wasn't sure what to do with his hands.
3. 2 are amazing, and the other is good
  - Meh F1 had great driving scenes but the story was just ok.
    - True, i was watching it, and it felt it was mish mash of other car movies.
    - Characters in F1 felt like they were written by 14 year olds. 

Visually it was a treat though.
4. Ford Vs Ferrari 🔥🔥
5. I want to go back and watch grand prix and le mans.
6. F1 wasn't near the level of Le Mans '66.

The former has nice racing scenes and that's it, while the latter is great in every aspect. Imo the best racing movie ever

## 4. D A R K

**Score:** 497

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. For me, one of the very best sci-fi series for YEARS! Thought provoking, intelligent, well constructed and filmed. Brilliant!
2. Great show, but how they manage to find actors that so perfectly match themselves younger/older is exceptional.
3. Explaining time loops >>> explaining why grandma is dating her grandson
4. First 2 season I was edge of my seat last 1 isn't bad it just feels flat. Like part of the fun was this constantly splitting mystery and while it was impressive they were able to tie up all the loose ends, it just didn't feel satisfying. Like I think I would have preferred they preserve a little more mystery instead of telling you how the sausage was made.
5. I loooved season 1.

Then season 2 got so convoluted I just stopped watching haha
  - same
6. I loved it!
7. Cant deny that and why I didnt recommend it to my mother, she will not understand.
8. Story didn’t make an arc, it formed an entire circle. ⭕️ Ending was phenomenal. 
I had to pick pen and paper to explain it to my friends.
9. Amazing show . Easier to watch if you follow on the wiki where it shows you diagrams of who’s related to whom , and it’s spoiler free based on which episode you are currently on .

## 5. Most terrifying death scenes?

**Score:** 243
**Body:** Anybody who has seen The Vanishing (1988) knows what I’m talking about when I say, pure nightmare fuel. A slow burn thriller with one of the best third acts I’ve ever seen and arguably the most horrifying death in movie history.

What other death scenes are terrifying? 

https://filmwaffle.com/post/one-of-the-most-chilling-mystery-movies-ive-ever-seen

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Lake Berryessa stabbings scene in Zodiac. The sound design, the way it’s shot, the lack of a score, and the fact that it’s real. Absolutely horrific.
  - Adding to this, with it also being in broad daylight is incredibly unsettling
2. 1917. When they save the German pilot from the burning plane, one goes to get water to douse the flames and he comes back to see the german stabbing his friend, 

Sobbed in the theater man
3. Saving Private Ryan stabbing scene
  - That was such a hard scene to watch. 
4. The scene where Andy Serkis is devoured by the giant penis monster in Peter Janckson’s King Kong
5. That movie scared the crap out of me. But the scariest death isn’t even a horror movie for me. It’s the animated shoe that Christopher Lloyd lowers into the dip in Roger Rabbit. That was traumatizing to a kid. 
6. Bone Tomahawk
  - Ehh, its decent but I'm split on that one
7. American History X. The curb scene.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. What moment/scene in a film did you react to in a way no one else did?

**Score:** 28
**Body:** I saw *Good Time* (Safdie Bros., 2017) opening weekend in a packed theater. When the credits rolled, the house lights came on and people casually got up to leave. The footage rolling behind the credits *wrecked* me. 

I couldn’t move. Absolutely inconsolable — sobbing, choking, shaking — while everyone else looked totally unfazed, looking at me like "the hell is wrong with that guy?"

What film moments hit you in a way no one else around you seemed to feel?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I saw *Happiness* in the theater. Very dark humor, and the audience was uncomfortable and quiet.

Dylan Baker plays a pedo and his son finds out and asks him, "Dad..? Would you fuck me?" Obviously concerned. Baker, in a very reassuring tone, says, "Nooo. I wouldn't fuck you, I would jerk off to you," or something like that.

I chuckled because that's really fucking dark, and you could hear it ring out in the dead silence of that theater.
  - Happiness is a top movie for me. The whole thing is so fucking dark but it is hilarious. I haven't seen it in a while, but was this a recent showing or when it first came out? I can imagine a crowd when it first came out being absolutely dead silent, no levity in the slightest
    - Yeah, it was in its first run. Late '90s? Crowd was not prepared lol.
  - The final scene in the movie, when the boy goes to his family to say "I came", made me laugh uncontrollably, the sheer absurdity of ending a movie in that note
2. Everyone else in the theater was laughing during the cheeseburger scene in The Menu, but I found it very touching. I mean, I do get why one would find it funny considering the circumstances, but the way it showed him returning to simple craft, feeling passion for his art in a way he had lost, I found it quite profound. But I feel like that entire movie is a bit of a rorschach test based on how you feel about pretentious art, so it will play a lot differently for varying audiences. 
  - I didn't see it in theaters but I don't think that scene was supposed to be funny, at least I didn't read it as funny either. Weird. I think the movie is very funny for most of it, though.
3. I'm not sure I can think of another movie that applies but Good Time also depressed me. I remember watching it alone in my crappy apartment feeling totally desolate after ward just pacing around in the TV light.
4. The Pursuit of Happyness.  It’s based on the supposedly true story of a poor single dad who pulled himself up by his bootstraps and got rich.  I don’t remember the details but I watched that movie thinking the MC was a shitty father and that a lot of the story had to have been made up.  Everyone else around me found it inspirational and heartwarming.
5. Man the ending to Good Time is one of the most gutwrenching endings ever, it wasn't in the cinema but I had the same thing happen when I watched it with a group of friends, they just didn't get it.


In cinema it was rolling laughing at the end of OUATIH after spending the whole movie dreading how the hell Tarantino was going to depict the actual murders, felt like DeNiro in Cape Fear 
6. Pickpocket, right after the main character defiantly declares his mother will fight and beat cancer the next scene is of him sitting silently in front of her casket in a funeral home. I bursted out howling with laughter. The rest of the theater? I swear it felt as though everyone turned their heads back at me with disapproval radiating from their eyes. I shrank down to two inches at that moment. But to be fair, it was pretty funny.

## 2. George Harrison: A Cinematic Catalog?

**Score:** 10
**Body:** “My Sweet Lord” showed up in *Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2*, and “Beware of Darkness” just popped up in *Weapons*. It hit me… George’s solo catalog is insanely cinematic.

*All Things Must Pass* alone could score an entire movie. Epic, sweeping, and emotional from start to finish. And then *Living in the Material World* brings in those intimate acoustic cuts that feel tailor-made for reflective, character-driven scenes.

Are we kind of sleeping on just how screen-ready Harrison’s music is? His guitar tones, spiritual themes, and layered arrangements feel like they were built for film.

* Which George Harrison solo tracks would fit perfectly into a movie scene?
* If you were a director, what kind of film would you score with *All Things Must Pass*?
* Do you think his catalog could inspire a full “jukebox” soundtrack film the way Elton John and Queen’s music have?
* Why do you think filmmakers don’t use his songs more often? Is it rights issues, or is he just underrated by Hollywood?
* Any dream directors you’d love to see pair with Harrison’s music?

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. His first solo album was literally a movie soundtrack.

He also founded Handmade Films, which produced a handful of films, many of which are considered classics or cult classics by now, like *Life of Brian*, *Time Bandits* or *Withnail and I*. And he had cameo appearances in a bunch of them.
  - Yes! I believe they are also responsible for releasing The Long Good Friday, a British classic
2. I always thought Art of Dying was a perfect Bond song, even down to the title.

I can't recall if it was used in the movie, but the trailer for Are You There God? It's Me Margaret used an arrangement of What is Life that I found incredibly charming.
3. Beware of Darkness is kind of a deep cut too, I remember hearing it and was surprised it wasn’t used in a Scorsese film or something, so he’s an easy choice, but he did make the doc. Loved weapons use!

## 3. Pulse (2001) and The Rules of Attraction (2002)

**Score:** 11
**Body:** I’ve always felt like movies about the college experience are totally lacking, especially considering the surplus of movies about high school. That said, I’ve since noticed a few connecting threads between two of my favorite movies that happen to take place during college.

I think the main idea that sets this off is kind of the basic thesis of both films. In The Rules of Attraction, the idea that no one will ever know anyone is pretty clearly stated. In pulse, it’s the experiment involving the cells which are pulled together if they’re too far apart, but destroy eachother if they come to close, that seem to represent the same basic idea.

Both movies are set in the university environment, but despite being set in completely different countries still manage to have this oppressive environment that emphasizes depression, suicide, and the bleakness that comes with encountering the world without the safety net of parents or family. Characters come in and out of eachother lives, and bounce off one and another without being any better for it at the end.

The movie (pulse) and the book (the rules of attraction) also both include a kind of last minute escape with a potential partner. At the middl-ish of pulse two of the central characters attempt to escape on a train, to no avail. Similarly, the book that rules of attraction is based on ends with a similarly ill-fated escape into married life and suburbia, for a time.

I don’t really have any clear conclusions to draw from this, so idk. But I guess I’m just drunk and looking to see what strangers on the internet think of this ridiculous connection I’ve found between these two movies I’m very passionate about.

What do you think?

Edit - grammar

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I was a senior in college when Rules of Attraction came out and I thought it was incredible. My then-girlfriend hated it. Never seen Pulse, and I haven’t watched Rules of Attraction in 20+ years, so I’d be interested in seeing how I feel about it now.
  - I rewatched *Rules of Attraction* a few months ago for the first time in like a decade and it holds up pretty well. It has surprisingly inspired editing and a great soundtrack.

Also Shannyn Sossoman has always been my main celebrity crush since I was a kid seeing her in *A Knights Tale*.
  - You should definitely check both of them out. As a college student now, rules of attraction definitely holds up.
2. somewhat tangential to your point, (I have read but not gotten to see Rules of Attraction) I have a question.. are there any US college films where people actually study things?  
The acquisition of knowledge doesn't necessarily lend itself to cinema but  I recall that John Houseman played a cranky law professor in the Paper Chase in the 70s where the exertions of the course where a focal point.

Often though Donna Tartt's novel The Secret History could make a good movie.
  - There’s a very good French film by Emmanuel Bourdieu called “Poison Friends” (2006) that’s about the students’ life of the mind. And it would also fall into the dark academia subgenre you’re citing with “The Secret History,” whose author Donna Tartt went to college at Bennington with Brett Easton Ellis, the author of “The Rules of Attraction.”
3. I know these are 2 pretty well-known movies, but the amount of intersection between these 2 films' audiences has got to be so miniscule, there's a chance you're the only person on the planet who's even SEEN both of them, much less calls them favorites. For that reason, your post might not get much interest; but because it's very imaginative, I fully support you
  - I guess I can’t fully call them both favorites, having only recently seen pulse in its entirety, but as a Bret Easton Ellis fan and a college student I can’t help but relate to the vibe that prevails throughout his works. The whole concept of these people who are just fumbling through life without any thought to how their decisions impact others comes through to a certain degree in Pulse. While it’s not 1:1, that concept of the hopelessness that can somehow break through during this weird period of life is so well presented in both movies that this tragedy of the mundane idea just shines through so clearly in both that it’s just kind of surprising to me
  - There are literally dozens of us!  Dozens!

I love both of of those movies, but I feel like OP is overselling the connection between them.

## 4. Robert Bresson's "Four Nights of a Dreamer" - Dialoque Question

**Score:** 10
**Body:** I recently saw and greatly enjoyed the new 4K restoration of Robert Bresson's *Four Nights of a Dreamer*, with English subtitles. I would like to cite some of the dialogue from the film for a personal project, but I don't speak French and don't have my own copy of the film to consult the French subtitles.

I am specifically thinking of the scene toward the very end of the film in which Jacques confesses to Marthe that he has indeed fallen for her. Marthe asks an agitated Jacques, "What's the matter?", to which he replies: "I love you. That's the matter." Shortly thereafter he lets out an exasperated, "What have you done to me?"

Would anyone here be so kind as to tell me what these lines are exactly in French? ("I love you. That's the matter," and, "What have you done to me?") I would be very grateful!

Also very happy to hear others' opinions of this film. I'm still processing my own thoughts about it and may try to catch it again while it's still at my local cinema. My initial reaction is that I'm quite taken by a film that is so intensely aware of the ridiculousness of this sort of romance and the tropes associated with it, but then so meticulously earns every ounce of genuine sentiment it can wring from its premise and execution.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 5. Do you think Jodorowsky was inspired by his contemporary Pier Paolo Pasolini?

**Score:** 5
**Body:** I am a fan of both the auteurs and , with all the differences, I have noticed some similarities between the Franco-Chilean and the Italian one. Yes Jodorowsky was mainly inspired by Dalì’s and Bunuel’s Surrealism while Pasolini mainly by Italian Neorealism (like Rossellini) but  both of them use a provoking narration (using violence, sex, especially homosexual and ephebophiliac ones, blasphemy and critique to the systems of power) and also Jodorowsky worked with other Italian artists and authors (like Franco Battiato). Do you think there might be any connection between the two?

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


---
